data.title: TLT Vs. VCTL: Yield Spread Is Near 10-Year Low
data.description:
TLT vs. VCLT ETF: previous thesis and new development

I last analyzed the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT) about 2 months ago in mid-March 2025. That article was titled “TLT Vs. EDV: Why I Prefer EDV And How I Allocate It Dynamically.” The article explained how we dynamically adjust the bond-equity ratio and rated TLT as a hold for the following considerations:

I use a dynamic allocation method based on bonds’ valuations relative to other assets. The current spread between SP500 CAPE yield and long-term treasury rates is toward the thinner end of the historical spectrum. This indicates attractive valuation of long-term bonds like those held in TLT and EDV relative to equity. Among these two ETFs, I prefer EDV due to its lower fees, which can more than offset its higher trading friction given my frequent portfolio adjustments.

As for the Vanguard Long-Term Corporate Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:VCLT), I last analyzed this ETF about 1 month ago in late March 2025. That article was titled “VGLT Vs. VCLT: This Chart Shows Now Is The Wrong Time For Corporate Bonds.” It rated VCLT as a sell after the following consideration of the credit spread between corporate bonds and risk-free rates:

VCLT presents an unattractive reward/risk profile compared to VGLT due to several considerations. The top one is that the credit spread between corporate bonds and treasury rates is near its thinnest level since the 1980s. In addition, VGLT could offer potential tax benefits for many investors.

Since the publication of those articles, the macroeconomic landscape has shifted materially and motivated a reexamination of both ETFs. More specifically, the tariff disputes that started on April 2, 2025 (aka the "Liberation Day") and also the inflation data released then have caused the market to reassess interest rate scenarios. As a result of these changes, the Federal Reserve’s latest decision (announced on Wednesday, May 7) was to keep Fed rates unchanged while issuing a caution that both unemployment and inflation could increase if tariff disputes escalate.

Under this backdrop, the thesis of this article is twofold. First, I will explain why the bond market is severely underestimating the risks behind corporate bonds such as those held in VCLT, given the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties. Second, I will explain that treasury bonds such as TLT offer one of the most attractive return/risk ratios in a decade relative to VCLT. With these considerations, this article maintains my hold rating on TLT and sell rating on VCLT.

TLT and VCLT ETF: basic facts

To facilitate the subsequent analyses, let me take a minute to go over the basics of both VCLT and TLT. As already stated in the names, both funds target the long end of the bond maturity spectrum. TLT, issued by BlackRock, focuses on treasury bonds with 20 years of remaining maturity or more, and VCLT is indexed on corporate bonds with maturities greater than 10 years as detailed in their fund description below.

TLT fund description: TLT tracks a market-weighted index of debt issued by the US Treasury with remaining maturities of 20 years or more. TLT effectively captures the far end of the Treasury curve in a liquid package. Exclusively holding bonds with 20+ years to maturity, TLT is by design very sensitive to long-term interest-rate movements. TLT is a great choice for investors who want long-term Treasury exposure.

VCLT fund description: VCLT holds a broad portfolio of long-term investment-grade corporate bonds. Like all long-term bond funds, it is highly sensitive to long-term interest rates, but its high weighted-average credit rating means that its credit risk is very small. The fund includes USD-denominated, fixed-rate, taxable securities issued by industrial, utility, and financial companies with more than 10 years until maturity.

Both funds are taxable ETFs, although note that dividend income from TLT could be exempted from state tax as federal obligations. TLT has a higher expense ratio of 0.15% with a much larger AUM of $47.01 billion. In contrast, VCLT features Vanguard’s signature lower expense ratio of 0.03%. It has a much smaller, but still sizable, AUM of $13.16 billion.

TLT-VCLT yield spread is near a 10-year low

To illustrate the risk premium of TLT relative to VCLT, the following chart displays the dividend yield spread between the VCLT and TLT in the past 10 years. Over the past decade, the spread has fluctuated widely, reaching a high of 2.929 in the wake of the pandemic and a low of 0.838 in 2024. But overall, the spread has been tractable and range-bound around an average of 1.664 most of the time as seen. The current spread is only 1.087, not only far below the average but also among the lowest levels in at least 10 years. I believe such a thin spread illustrates an unusually unfavorable return/risk ratio for VCLT relative to treasury bonds.

As to be further elaborated next, VCLT valuation risks are further heightened by its credit exposure and also the macroeconomic uncertainties.

Other risks and final thoughts

The chart below shows a breakdown of the credit exposure of VCLT as of March 31, 2025. To echo a point mentioned earlier, the exposure is predominantly in investment-grade corporate bonds. The largest allocation is to BBB-rated bonds, making up 45.00% of the fund. Bonds rated A constituted the next largest portion at 43.50%. Higher-rated bonds comprise a minor portion of the funds with AA-rated bonds at 9.30%, AAA bonds at 2.10%, and a minimal exposure of 0.20% to U.S. Government securities.

The significant exposure to medium-quality corporate debt within the VCLT portfolio, when combined with the fund’s long duration, could cause large downside corrections in the case of a market downturn. If the overall economy weakens (for example, as a result of escalated tariff disputes), I expect the companies with weaker credit ratings to be impacted more severely due to their limited financial resources.

On the positive side, VCLT’s diversification offers some degree of protection against such downside. As seen in the next chart, TLT only holds 43 securities, with a significant 47.94% of its assets concentrated in its top 10 holdings. Investors have no need to be alarmed by such concentrated exposure. TLT focuses on treasury bonds only, and the credit risk of one treasury bond is the same as the credit risk of all treasury bonds. In contrast, VCLT is much more diversified, holding 2790 securities to diversify the credit risks. As seen, it has only 2.60% of its assets invested in its top 10 holdings.

data.title: Champion Homes: Sitting On The Sidelines
data.description:
Nobody has a perfect track record when it comes to investing. But I feel like I have a pretty solid one. One company that I could point to as an example of this is Champion Homes (NYSE:SKY), an enterprise that produces manufactured homes from the manufacturing facilities that it operates. This is an interesting niche of the housing market that's often overlooked by investors. The reason why I say I have a good track record with this business is because of how my rating calls on it have played out over time. You see, whenever I rate a company a "hold," I'm making the claim that I believe that shares are unlikely to perform materially different than the broader market for the foreseeable future. And that's precisely what has been the case here.

Since my last article about the business in October of last year, an article in which I reaffirmed the company as a "hold" candidate, shares have pulled back by 2.6%. This is not materially different than the 3.2% drop the S&P 500 saw. And since I originally rated it a "hold" in October 2022, the stock has soared by 55.6%. But that's barely any different than the 56.4% that the broader market experienced. Looking at the picture today, I see little reason to change my assessment. Shares are still quite pricey compared to many other homebuilders. The firm does have a great balance sheet, but that's not enough to justify matters. Add on top of this concerns about the economy because of the current trade war situation, and I believe that maintaining my current "hold" rating is appropriate.

This does not mean that I can't or won't change my opinion at some point. As new data comes in, I would be crazy not to if it’s warranted. It just so happens that, on May 20, after the market closes, the management team at this homebuilder will be reporting financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts anticipate revenue and profits all rising nicely year over year. But unless we see something really special happen, I don't see an upgrade as likely.

Still Interesting, Still Pricey

The manufactured housing industry is interesting to me in part because it's a battle between two concepts. The first of these is that because the homes are substantially cheaper than traditional homes, they're properties that technically should appeal to lower income Americans. To see the extent to which this is true, we need only consider that the median sales price for a new house in the U.S. in March of this year was $403,600. That was down from the $436,400 experienced one year earlier, with high interest rates and inflationary pressures hammering demand. To put this in perspective, in the third quarter of its 2025 fiscal year, Champion Homes reported that the average price of a home that it sold in the U.S. was only $94,900. That's a substantial difference that should appeal to those on the lower end of the income and wealth spectrum.

The other opposing concept is that lower income Americans are the least likely to be able to afford purchasing a home anyways. According to Urban Wire, the home ownership rate for the lowest income quintile in the U.S. was 45.2% in 2023. That compares to 65.9% for those in the middle quintile and 85.3% for those in the top. The company thrives by finding that sizable minority of the lowest income Americans and catering to them.

According to management, because of how expensive homes are, 60% of the U.S. population cannot afford a traditional home. This is problematic for those who, like me, advocate for generational wealth-building opportunities. It's also worth pointing out that this is particularly problematic when it comes to younger Americans. Using data from 2021, management showed that 46% of all manufactured homes are owned by Millennials. However, because of lower income in retirement, Baby Boomers represent the second-largest cohort of 26%.

Long term, management seems optimistic about this industry. And this is because there has been considerable under-building in this space since the 1990s. There was a big drop from 1998 until 2009 because of the housing bubble that formed. And ever since that popped, manufactured housing shipments have been on the rise. They still, however, lag significantly behind what they were from the early 1960s through the late 1990s.

The growth experienced in this space has made it possible for Champion Homes to do quite well. Take its most recent quarter, which would be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, as an example. During that time, revenue for the business was $644.9 million. That's an increase of 15.3% compared to the $559.5 million reported one year earlier. Some of this increase was because of a rise in the average price per home that the company sold in the U.S.

That number grew from $92,300 to $94,900. Furthermore, the number of homes delivered in the U.S. expanded from 5,643 to 6,437 during this time. Unfortunately, the number of homes sold in Canada dropped from 249 to 209, while the average price there pulled back from $123,700 to $122,900. But as you can tell, Canada is a much less significant place for Champion Homes and its revenue stream.

The rise in revenue for the company has brought with it higher profits as well. Net income jumped from $47 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $61.5 million in the third quarter of this year. Operating cash flow did drop from $89.5 million to $50.5 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $59.8 million to $76 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $66.3 million to $83.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits and cash flows all improved year over year. The one exception was operating cash flow. But the adjusted figure for it and EBITDA both increased. This came as the number of homes delivered in the U.S. increased and as average pricing grew as well, both of which can be seen in the chart below. All things considered, the picture is looking quite positive.

In this environment, there's definitely uncertainty because of the tariff situation. According to the National Association of Home Builders, the tariffs currently imposed should add around $10,900 to a new homes’ typical construction costs. That number should certainly be lower for the manufactured housing industry because of the smaller size of those homes, and the lower quality materials that they are built with. In its third quarter earnings call, the company did not provide much in the way of detail. All they said was that their margin expectations for the final quarter of the year excluded the potential impact of tariffs. They also mentioned that they are remaining agile during this time and will keep investors updated as the picture changes.

I would venture to say that the firm's margins will probably be impacted some. But I suspect that they will pass on most of the costs onto the buyers of the properties. The way I view this situation is that while there could be a reduction in housing demand in general, including in the manufactured housing industry, what a prolonged period of economic weakness might do is merely shift up the average income of buyers of these properties. This means that those making more money, preferring to buy a cheaper home than a traditional home, will ultimately opt for a manufactured house instead of a traditional one. Those at the lowest end of the income stream, however, will certainly fall off and have to find alternative arrangements.

At present, analysts seem optimistic. After the market closes on May 20, management is going to be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Analysts currently believe that revenue will be around $595.4 million. That would be up from the $536.4 million reported last year and would likely come from additional unit shipments. They also anticipate earnings per share of $0.77, which would translate to a pretty nice rise over the $0.05 per share reported for the same time last year. This would mean a jump in net income from $2.8 million to $44.7 million.

On an adjusted basis, analysts believe that earnings per share will also be $0.77. That's more than the $0.62 per share in adjusted net income reported last year and would translate to a rise in adjusted net profits from $36 million to $44.7 million. In the table above, you can see what the other important cash flow metrics for the company were in the final quarter of last year. It is highly probable that these will also improve. However, I actually think that there's a chance that operating cash flow might actually drop. I say this because I think that management might have worked hard to stock up on building materials leading up to the implementation of tariffs. I don't have any evidence to support this. But it would be a logical decision.

In terms of valuation, the company is still quite a bit higher than I would like it to be. If we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would expect net profits of $165.3 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $245.5 million, and EBITDA of $297.2 million. Using these estimates and the historical performance for 2024, you can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. This pricing is indicative of a company that, in my book, is pretty solidly fairly valued. But compared to other homebuilders, most of which are admittedly traditional ones instead of manufactured ones, shares look pricey. In the table below, you can see what I mean. On a price to earnings basis, Champion Homes ended up being the most expensive of the six companies shown in that table. On a price to operating cash flow basis, three of the companies were cheaper than it is. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, this number increased to four.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Champion Homes 29.9 20.2 14.8
M/I Homes (MHO) 5.8 24.1 4.4
Tri Pointe Homes (TPH) 7.0 5.7 5.2
KB Home (KBH) 6.6 12.9 6.7
Cavco Industries (CVCO) 25.5 27.2 16.3
Dream Finders Homes (DFH) 6.6 16.5 7.2

Takeaway

As things stand, I do think that Champion Homes is an interesting business and I fully expect it to continue to grow as time goes on. Unfortunately, shares look more expensive than I would like them to be, even if we assume that attractive growth continues as it has. When you add on top of this the risks facing the homebuilding industry, I believe that keeping the business rated a "hold" is the right choice.

data.title: Aviat Networks: Decent Quarter, But Muted Near-Term Outlook - Buy (Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have discussed Aviat Networks, Inc. or “Aviat Networks” (NASDAQ:AVNW) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier coverage of the company.

Earlier this week, leading wireless transport and access solutions provider Aviat Networks reported strong Q3/FY2025 results, with both the company's top- and bottom line coming in well above consensus expectations:

While revenues were down on a sequential basis due to lower contributions from North America, revenues derived from regions like Europe and Africa/Middle East increased to new multi-year highs:

Adjusted EBITDA of $14.9 million represented a new record for the company while non-GAAP operating expenses declined to the lowest level since the close of the Pasolink acquisition 18 months ago.

However, free cash flow was slightly negative due to unfavorable working capital movements partially as a result of the decision to increase inventory in anticipation of higher tariffs.

Aviat Networks ended the quarter with $49.4 million in cash and cash equivalents as well as $73.9 million in debt. Please note that the Pasolink acquisition remains subject to a post-closing working capital adjustment which is estimated to result in a $13.2 million cash payment to the seller.

On the conference call, management attributed the impressive margin performance to strong software sales but warned of lower contributions in the final quarter of the company's fiscal year.

In combination with the impact of recently introduced tariffs, I would expect non-GAAP gross margins to dip below the 35% mark in Q4.

While the company reiterated full-year projections, management hinted to revenues likely coming in closer to the lower end of the provided range, very much in line with muted consensus expectations.

During the questions-and-answers session of the call, analysts were looking to get more color on the potential tariffs impact:

(...) And then in terms of the effects, in order to put an illustrative upper bound on the exposure, we think in the near-term, it could be as high as roughly somewhere between 2% and 2.5% of our COGS. But as of this very moment, we're working hard with our supply base to minimize those effects. And at the same time, we're committed to passing on to our customer base only what we can't mitigate ourselves.

So (...) linking it back to gross margins in the immediate term, this may cause a little bit of gross margin rate pressure for owners, but we do not anticipate any per share earnings leakage, widening the aperture though, over the long term, thinking about treating all our constituents as fairly as possible and like partners, we think will compound to longer-term benefits to owners. So that's what we're doing on tariffs and a little bit of the spin through the P&L and how it may affect.

While expectations for limited tariffs impact are good news, increased uncertainties have caused me to slightly reduce my profitability estimates and take a more conservative valuation approach.

Consequently, I have reduced my assigned EV/Adjusted EBITDA multiple from 10x to 8x and lowered my price target from $46.00 to $35.00.

With closest peer Ceragon Networks (CRNT) now offering higher upside, I am downgrading the company's shares from "Strong Buy" to "Buy."

Bottom Line:

Aviat Networks reported better-than-expected Q3/FY2025 results with solid revenue upside and strong profitability. However, full-year revenues are now expected to come in at the lower end of the company's guidance range.

With tariffs likely to result in some near-term margin pressure and more cautious customer buying patterns, I have reduced my profitability estimates and taken a more conservative valuation approach.

Consequently, I have lowered my price target from $46.00 to $35.00 and downgraded the company's shares from "Strong Buy" to "Buy."

data.title: Athabasca Oil: Benefitting From The Trans Mountain Expansion Pipeline Startup
data.description:
Athabasca Oil Corporation (OTCPK:ATHOF) is a thermal oil producer benefitting from the Trans Mountain Expansion Pipeline startup. This startup has at least temporarily reduced the discount between light oil and heavy oil in Canada. It also enabled this producer to conclude that its heavy oil operations now breakeven in the WTI low $30's area. However, this could prove to be a temporary situation if all the heavy oil producers expand production to restore the old relationship of heavy oil to light oil. This may take some years. But it is a long-term threat to thermal oil profitability.

Headline

Thermal oil prices are a bright spot in an industry worried about future commodity prices.

Athabasca Oil Announces 2025 First Quarter Results Highlighted by 63% Growth in Funds Flow Per Share and Strong Operational Execution Driving a Robust Return of Capital Program.

As the headline from the first quarter earnings press release strongly implies, the discount to light oil decreased to bring about a significant improvement in the discounted selling price situation. This largely increased the profitability of selling anything that could potentially be shipped through the expansion pipeline project that began operations.

Most of the time, thermal operations were by their nature low cost. The problem has always been that the discount to WTI light oil benchmark prices would expand during periods of weak pricing. That had the effect of raising the WTI price needed for the product to breakeven.

The startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion Pipeline Project has temporarily decreased that discount. Therefore, thermal oil producers will be reporting some atypically large margins for the near future.

However, the commodity business is very predictable in that it is in all the thermal producers' interest to produce more thermal oil in this situation. Therefore, count on this part of the industry to increase production until the old discount relationships are restored.

It will take time for that production increase to happen. But expansion plans are already appearing in the companies I follow. Therefore, investors should not expect the current situation to be a long-term opportunity.

Financial Results

Sure enough, earnings per share roughly doubled the previous fiscal year.

(Note: Athabasca Oil Is a Canadian Company That Reports Using Canadian Dollars Unless Otherwise Noted.)

Free cash flow and GAAP cash flow works a bit differently for thermal oil companies because thermal projects often have a relatively large upfront cost. That creates a sizable depreciation component to enable more cash flow than usual. Since thermal production typically declines slowly, more of that cash flow becomes free cash flow than is typical for the unconventional oil producers.

Thermal producers also free cash flow and cash flow generously when losing money because of the high upfront costs that need to be recovered over the life of the project.

The thermal group of producers may report a very good year due to the startup of the Trans Mountain Pipeline expansion project. This has drained a lot of supply out of the market for export purposes. At least temporarily, supply of this product is on the tighter side than it has been in years as a result. That makes this area a bright spot in the industry right now.

Leverage

As can be seen from the above slide, net debt is near zero just considering the debt noted above. Usually negative working capital (if it exists in any quarter) is caused by accruals made and is likewise not a worry.

However, this company usually chooses to maintain a sizable cash balance because it does deals from time to time. So, the leverage situation could quickly change simply by spending that cash balance.

Cash is also necessary in case thermal prices weaken to the point where production needs to be shut-in to avoid negative cash flow until a pricing recovery begins. That may not be a threat this time around with lower discounts. But it is a long-term consideration.

Operations

Due to the issues of a widening discount during cyclical downturns, both heavy oil operators and thermal oil operators rarely survive long-term as independents.

This company formed a partnership with Cenovus Energy (CVE) to develop some light oil prospects. Light oil would provide some reliable cash flow during cyclical downturns when the thermal oil discount can widen without a pipeline startup.

In this case, the company may use the next few years to expand the partnership operations to the point where adequate light oil cash flow is obtained. Long-time readers know that this is the company's second attempt at this solution using a partnership. So let us see if this time works better than the first time a few years back.

Last Article

The last article reviewed the 2025 guidance and the immediate strategy for the partnership with Cenovus Energy.

The current period of uncertainty caused by the threat of tariffs and a trade war could render that article useless at this point.

Most likely what will happen is that most Canadian companies have low operating levels during Spring Breakup. Therefore, much of the article will likely be reviewed by management for an update that takes into account the current ongoing negotiations between the two companies.

We can likely expect something solid to use before operations startup again in the third quarter, as this situation is unlikely to persist that long.

Summary

Athabasca Oil is a thermal operator with the potential to add some light oil production. That may well enable the company to survive as an independent long-term. In the meantime, all the good news is out about the benefits of some export capacity. Therefore, further cyclical increases in the stock price are highly unlikely.

It is very likely that the industry will do what management is doing here to expand production. That will likely restore the discounted relationship that existed previously to the startup of the pipeline expansion project.

The unusual aspect of this company is the existence of a way to obtain substantial light oil production before that old discounted heavy oil relationship comes back. That makes this a highly speculative idea. That is especially true since this management is a bit more likely than other managements to buy and sell businesses.

Risks

This company keeps more debt than many thermal oil producers keep. Now there is a considerable cash balance that offsets that debt (mostly). However, this company has a tendency to buy and sell acreage. Therefore, the leverage could quickly change if management sees a deal it likes. Therefore, the existence of that cash and the debt represents an unusual potential leverage risk.

The thermal business also has the risk of the discount to light oil expanding during cyclical business downturns when commodity prices are weak.

Right now, that risk is mitigated by the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion Project. But with all the thermal oil expansion projects I am reading about, that benefit may prove to be an intermediate term benefit rather than a long-term benefit.

A loss of key personnel could prove to be a major setback to the future of the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Share Class Inception: A, I - 5/29/15; F - 2/28/17. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 5/29/15. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: Bloomberg Municipal Bond Short 1-5 Year Index measures the performance of municipal bonds with time to maturity of more than one year and less than five years. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

BLOOMBERG® and any Bloomberg Index are service marks of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates, including Bloomberg Index Services Limited ("BISL"), the administrator of the indices (collectively, "Bloomberg") and have been licensed for use for certain purposes by Hartford Funds. Bloomberg is not affiliated with Hartford Funds, and Bloomberg does not approve, endorse, review, or recommend any Hartford Funds product. Bloomberg does not guarantee the timeliness, accurateness, or completeness of any data or information relating to Hartford Funds products.

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed US Treasuries as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index returned 0.39% during the quarter. The ratio of 10-year AAA general obligations (GOs) to 10-year Treasuries increased from 68.4% to 77.4%, remaining below the long-term historical average of 88% over the last 20 years.

Performance Summary

The Hartford Municipal Short Duration Fund (I Share) underperformed the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Short 1-5 Year Index over the quarter.

Security selection in investment-grade and high-yield revenue bonds contributed positively to relative returns.

Security selection within GO bonds was also additive.

Duration* and yield positioning detracted as the Fund held a short-duration posture, specifically at the 2-year and 3-year segments of the yield curve, where yields tightened over the period.

Positioning & Outlook

Fundamentals are broadly positive after generous direct and indirect Federal support and coming out of the pandemic.

Many municipal sectors have natural outlets for inflationary pressures, but the more labor- constrained sectors will be more challenged.

Expect municipals to behave defensively in a downturn given their high quality and strong balance sheets.

Tax-exempt municipal valuations as proxied by the Muni/TSY ratio are unattractive relative to history. However, municipal bond all-in yields look attractive relative to comparable quality corporates at intermediate and long maturities.

Despite higher issuance this year, we expect demand to remain robust, particularly if rates begin to trend lower.

*Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of an investment’s price to nominal interest-rate movement.

1Expenses are from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication. Gross expenses do not reflect contractual expense reimbursement arrangements. Net expenses reflect such arrangements in instances when they reduce gross expenses. These arrangements remain in effect until 2/28/26 unless the Fund's Board of Directors approves an earlier termination. Without these arrangements, performance would have been lower.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • Municipal securities may be adversely impacted by state/local, political, economic, or market conditions. Investors may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax as well as state and local income taxes. Capital gains, if any, are taxable. • Fixed income security risks include credit, liquidity, call, duration, and interest-rate risk. As interest rates rise, bond prices generally fall. • Investments in high-yield ("junk") bonds involve greater risk of price volatility, illiquidity, and default than higher-rated debt securities.

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed US Treasuries as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond 1-15 Year Blend (1-17) Index returned 0.39% during the quarter. The ratio of 10-year AAA general obligations (GOs) to 10-year Treasuries increased from 68.4% to 77.4%, remaining below the long-term historical average of 88% over the last 20 years.

Performance Summary

The Hartford Sustainable Municipal Bond Fund (I Share) outperformed the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index over the period.

The primary contributor to relative returns was security selection in investment-grade (IG) and high-yield revenue bonds, particularly in the healthcare, education, and utilities sectors.

The Fund’s security selection in GO bonds, specifically in local GOs, positively impacted performance over the period.

Duration* and yield-curve positioning contributed to performance, as we maintained an overweight position in the 10-year segment of the curve, where yields rose in the AAA Municipal GO curve.

Our underweight allocation in IG revenue bonds, specifically in port, airport, and marina modestly detracted from benchmark-relative returns.

Positioning & Outlook

Fundamentals are broadly positive after generous direct and indirect Federal support and coming out of the pandemic.

Many municipal sectors have natural outlets for inflationary pressures, but the more labor- constrained sectors will be more challenged.

Expect municipals to behave defensively in a downturn given their high quality and strong balance sheets.

Tax-exempt municipal valuations as proxied by the Muni/TSY ratio are unattractive relative to history. However, municipal bond all-in yields look attractive relative to comparable quality corporates at intermediate and long maturities.

Despite higher issuance this year, we expect demand to remain robust, particularly if rates begin to trend lower.

*Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of an investment’s price to nominal interest-rate movement.

1Expenses are from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication. Gross expenses do not reflect contractual expense reimbursement arrangements. Net expenses reflect such arrangements in instances when they reduce gross expenses. These arrangements remain in effect until 2/28/26 unless the Fund's Board of Directors approves an earlier termination. Without these arrangements, performance would have been lower.

data.title: Zoetis: Solid Q1 Results And A Weaker Dollar Provide Support (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Zoetis (NYSE:ZTS) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, losing about 8% of their value. Since peaking in late 2024, shares have dropped about 20%. This adds to a period of long-term underperformance. I last covered shares back in 2022, rating the stock a “sell” on valuation concerns. Since then, ZTS has returned just 8% while the market has gained over 50%. However, periods of underperformance can come to an end. This week, Zoetis delivered solid earnings results and guidance. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if there is value opportunity in Zoetis. I am no longer a seller.

In the company’s first quarter, Zoetis earned $1.48, beating estimates by $0.08 on revenue of $2.2 billion, flat from last year. EPS was up 8% from last year, aided by a ~2% lower share count due to stock repurchases. Organic growth was up 9% given 5% volume growth and a 4% increase in prices. However, the stronger dollar was a significant headwind given the company gets over 40% of its revenue from overseas. That pressure will reverse substantially as the US dollar has weakened significantly since President Trump announced widespread tariffs. The dollar is down over 7% from its highs, which will mechanistically boost results of multinational companies like Zoetis.

Foreign exchange and tariffs create a lot of movement in Zoetis’s financials that are essentially out of its control. However, the company is managing its own operations fairly well. Even as revenue rose by 1%, the cost of sales fell by 3%, enabling 140bps of gross margin expansion. SG&A was up 2% to $554 million while it trimmed R&D spending by 2% to $157 million. I would not want to see R&D fall much further to ensure it continues investing sufficiently in its pipeline while SG&A cost control remains positive.

Looking at geographic results, its US business is about 55% of the company and saw 6% organic growth. Its pet business saw 8% growth to $965 million while livestock fell by 2% to $210 million. US gross margins expanded 190bs to 83.2%. Livestock was down an unadjusted 19% due to divestitures. A relatively weak pricing environment for livestock manufacturing is likely to keep this part of the business under pressure. Zoetis did receive a conditional license for its Avian flu vaccine in the US and Canada, which could provide some tailwind.

Its international segment saw 11% organic growth as its livestock unit performed much better than in the US, gaining 12% to $400 million. Foreign exchange was a 7% headwind to international results, which is why reported revenues were flat. Given the swing in the US dollar, this headwind should turn into a tailwind during the second quarter. International margins expanded 110bps to 70% and run persistently lower than the US given the higher of livestock business.

In terms of international exposure, I would note that China is just 6% of sales at $60 million. This was a 20% decline from last year. Conversely, Canada was the standout, growing by 17% to $67 million. I view the China tariffs and trade relationship as likely to be the most problematic, so this market will likely continue to be weak. I am also cautious that deeply damaged US-Canadian relations could cause growth to decelerate here.

Looking at its key franchises, its Simparica business continues to benefit from increased adoption of its “trio” product, benefitting from being the only dog parasiticide that can prevent flea tapeworms. It also gained approval in South Korea. This product group now accounts for about 18% of the company, growing 19%. Its Dermatology business was also up a solid 10%

Its osteoarthritis business is fairly small but is also growing very quickly. As the dog population ages, the potential cohort increases, and just 20% of dogs receive treatment. This is an area I am cautious on. As we have seen over the years, consumers, especially in the United States, love to spend money on their pets. However, in an environment where consumer sentiment is weakening and discretionary income may face pressure, we could see some consumers pull back. Products like anti-flea medication are likely to be resilient, but more discretionary “quality of life” treatments, like for OA, could see slower take-up in a weaker economy.

Because so much of its business is overseas, the weakness in the US dollar provides a significant opportunity, driving the $200 million increase in revenue guidance. I would note that it sees a 1% headwind from enacted tariffs to net income, suggesting it does not expect to fully pass through costs. It is important to note that this includes just enacted tariffs with most reciprocal tariffs under a 90 day pause. While there are likely to be some trade deals, tariffs will likely rise on at least some companies, meaning we may see future margin pressure. Even with slower adjusted net income growth, I believe EPS will be $0.20 higher, thanks to the FX tailwind.

In terms of Zoetis’s exposure to tariffs, it sees $20 million of tariff headwinds as explained on the earnings call. Its primary tariff exposure is livestock products from China. It sees greater risk from retaliatory tariffs. That is because 60% of its manufacturing is inside the US, and it is a net exporter. Most imports and exports are to Europe, where tariff rates are lower for now and never likely to reach the 145% China level. Separately, the Administration is considering sectoral tariffs on pharmaceuticals. It is not clear whether pharmaceutical tariffs will include pet products, but is hopeful they can be avoided. The motivation here does not seem as focused on pet medications, in my view.

I understand why the company is not issuing guidance beyond enacted tariffs as the future policy stance is unknowable. I expect the ultimate tariff impact to be 2-3x the current policy stance, creating another ~$40 million or $0.08 of risk to EPS. That actually still leaves the company a net beneficiary of the trade war, given the impact of the lower US dollar is likely to be over $0.20.

Zoetis also maintains an excellent balance sheet, ending Q1 with $1.7 billion of cash and just $6.6 billion of debt. That net debt position is just above 2x its $2.3 billion of 2024 free cash flow. As such, I expect all free cash flow to go to shareholders via its buyback 1.3% dividend yield. Indeed, there were over $400 million of Q1 buybacks.

Ultimately, the business continues to perform well, and Zoetis is a primary beneficiary of a weaker US dollar, more than offsetting any tariff costs. Even with incremental tariffs, I expect EPS to be at least $6.10 and for at least $2.4 billion of free cash flow. Shares are trading at about 25x earnings with a ~3.4% free cash flow yield ($2.4 billion of free cash flow over a ~$70 billion market cap). This is a high-margin business with excellent free cash flow that should benefit from increased spending on pets over time.

Still, this is not cheap, and ZTS shares have been a victim of excessive valuation. While shares are trading at a large discount to their own history, they remain very expensive relative to peers. I do believe this does limit ZTS’s upside potential, especially is discretionary consumer spending slows.

Given secular tailwinds, I do think ZTS does merit some premium valuation, especially as it has an under-levered balance sheet, which could allow it to increase buybacks further if shares continue to underperform. Over the medium term, ZTS should be able to grow by at least 5% per year in a constant currency environment, so a starting 3.4% free cash flow yield and 5% growth provide scope for an 8+% return, as ZTS can return all free cash flow to investors via dividends and buybacks given its strong balance sheet.

I struggle to get excited about this valuation, but I don’t think shares merit a sell anymore. I see ZTS as likely to be a market performer and hold up relatively well if tariffs escalate. If the dollar continues to weaken, that could open the door to more upside. Overall, ZTS is performing well, but valuation reflects this appropriately, leaving shares as a hold at current levels.
